questions.question: What does `margin: 10px 20px` set?
choices: 10px on all sides, 20px on left only
questions.explanation: The 2-value margin shorthand sets top/bottom to the first value and left/right to the second value. So margin: 10px 20px means 10px vertical and 20px horizontal.

questions.question: When two vertical margins of 20px and 30px meet between adjacent block elements, what is the resulting space?
questions.explanation: This is margin collapsing: when vertical margins of adjacent block elements overlap, only the larger margin is applied. The 30px margin wins, not 20px + 30px.

questions.question: What does `margin: 10px 20px 30px 40px` apply?
questions.explanation: The 4-value shorthand follows clockwise order: top, right, bottom, left (think of a clock starting at 12). This is sometimes remembered as TRouBLe.

questions.question: What effect does a negative margin have on an element?
choices: It is ignored by the browser
It pulls the element in the direction of the negative margin
It doubles the margin on the opposite side
questions.explanation: Negative margins pull the element toward the direction of the negative value. For example, margin-top: -10px pulls the element upward by 10px, potentially overlapping elements above it.

questions.question: The phenomenon where adjacent vertical margins combine into a single margin is called margin ___.
questions.explanation: Margin collapsing occurs when vertical margins of adjacent block-level elements overlap. The larger margin value is used rather than adding them together.

questions.question: The property ___ sets only the space above an element.
questions.explanation: The individual margin properties (margin-top, margin-right, margin-bottom, margin-left) set spacing on specific sides, unlike the shorthand which sets multiple sides at once.
